reasoning: Since hitting 0.72 in 2023, South Korea's fertility trends have shown signs of an impending short-term recovery. In mid-2024, the country experienced a strong surge in marriages—with monthly numbers growing 10-20% year-on-year—largely driven by couples catching up on delayed pandemic plans. Monthly birth totals also broke a long downward streak, increasing year-on-year in Q3 2024. Because most first births in South Korea occur 1 to 2 years after marriage, the 2024 marriage boomlet will primarily elevate birth numbers throughout 2025. Additionally, the shrinking denominator (the population of women of childbearing age) exerts an upward mathematical pressure on the TFR even if absolute birth numbers only remain flat. Although the December 2024 martial law turbulence introduces some uncertainty, it is functionally too brief and late to reverse the structural fertility momentum built up over early-to-mid 2024. Consequently, a 2025 TFR greater than 0.72 is more likely than not.
